So what you find here is the use of the free writing test, down in your right hand corner of that ad, the free writing test, which then enables them to come back to you and say, good news, you passed the test. You have enormous latent talent. This is perfect for you. So the proof, the explanation of the opportunity is helped by the test and the proof of personal attainability is in the test. How many of you, you guys have all seen the print ads of the TV commercials for the, we will market your invention for you, guys, right? But it works, and it works fabulous, and the average sales is 5, 8, 10 grand. And so if you want to play prospect, so you have to send in, say send you stuff, and then you send back in a little description, you fill out a form of what your proposed invention is. And if you want to play prospect, go make up the dumbest damn thing you can think of, right? Just the stupidest thing you can think of, right? Because you will get the call telling you that their invention evaluation committee has decided, yours is the most amazing thing they have ever heard of. And industry will be salivating all over itself, competing to get the rights to your combination portable urinal for women and Bose stereo system, all in the same day, are going to tell you it's the most brilliant thing they've ever heard of. It's a version of this, okay? It is proof by passing the test. So it's proof of personal attainability in an environment where belief in personal attainability is exceptionally low. The other answer to this is analogy. So if you flip to your page is 187, 188, and so forth. So if I... How many in here have eaten rattlesnake? Some? Good, okay. But most of you have not eaten rattlesnake. You want to know what rattlesnake tastes like? Tastes like chicken. So how many of you have eaten fried vulture? Want to know what vulture tastes like? Chicken. You want to know what the most objectionable food you could think of that you would never want to eat in a million years tastes like when somebody is trying to get you to eat it? It tastes like chicken. Tastes like chicken, right? Fried shit on a stick. I don't want to eat that. Oh, you'll like it tastes just like chicken, right? That's what everybody says, right? And by the way, quite a bit of it does not taste like chicken at all. And furthermore, my theory is if it tastes like chicken, why the hell don't we eat chicken? There's like a KFC on the corner, right? I mean, we sort of know where that came from, right? Well, the reason people do that to you when they are trying to get you to eat some weird damn thing you don't want to eat is because it's an analogy everybody accepts. Tastes like chicken. Oh, okay. And they eat it. So that's what you need to put across a difficult proposition that people do not understand or may not believe. You need a tastes like chicken analogy for it. You need to link it to something that they already understand, they already get, and they at least don't find objectionable in the case of the food or better yet they like and would want to own. So two examples I gave you is oil wells. I happen to use a lot as an analogy because really everybody thinks that it'd be pretty damn cool to own an oil well, right? And they get it. You got an oil well over here. You turn it on, it just keeps pumping. Sometimes I talk about the little gas wells. You know, if you drive across Texas, everybody's got one in their front yard because the company will put it in there for you and they send you a check every month, right? And it just, you turn it on once and it just keeps pumping and every month you get a check. Well, it'd be cool to have and most people wouldn't object to having it in their front yard. Put a few bushes around it, nobody notices, and it comes to check. So everybody gets that thing and it'd be cool. In the marketing to your Fluid book ad I gave you here, the analogy is to a place, right? So the affluent as a market, not so easy to wrap your head around. A place easier to wrap your head around. So you always want, when you are presenting opportunity, particularly one, that may be a little difficult for people to wrap their head around. You want a taste like chicken, right? If you notice in the U.S. Gold Show, we several times referred to it as a portable store. So that's a taste like chicken. In the early days of Get Rich in Internet, several companies, including Guthrie Wrenker, two more minutes, I'm going to tell you a break. But so the last example, made an enormous amount of money, direct mail mostly, and seminars, people in the rooms, selling Internet store fronts in an Internet mall. So you may remember iMall was the biggest player in this category. Guthrie Wrenker was in the business and there were several others. So the story, they literally sold pieces of paper for $3,000. So the story went like this. And of course the story is ill. But the story is, we got this mall. You all know what a mall is. So we got a mall on the Internet. It's just like the mall in your area except a ton more people come to it and it doesn't have any walls. But it's a mall. And in that mall, you can have a store except you don't have to have a physical store, you don't have to have a bunch of inventory, you don't have to have a bunch of employees, but you can have a store.
